questions.question: The Doppler effect is the change in observed:
options: Amplitude of a wave due to relative motion
Frequency of a wave due to relative motion
Speed of a wave due to relative motion
Wavelength of a wave due to temperature
questions.explanation: The Doppler effect is the apparent change in frequency (and wavelength) caused by relative motion between the source and observer.

questions.question: As a sound source moves toward a stationary observer, the observed frequency:
questions.explanation: When the source approaches, wavefronts are compressed, reducing wavelength and increasing the observed frequency (higher pitch).

questions.question: As a sound source moves away from a stationary observer, the observed frequency:
questions.explanation: When the source moves away, wavefronts are stretched, increasing wavelength and decreasing the observed frequency (lower pitch).

questions.question: An ambulance siren sounds higher in pitch as it approaches and lower as it moves away. This is explained by:
options: The reflection of sound off buildings
The increase in sound speed near the ambulance
The resonance of the siren with the air
questions.explanation: The change in pitch is the classic example of the Doppler effect — frequency shifts due to relative motion between source and observer.

questions.question: As a sound source moves forward, the wavelengths in front of the source become:
questions.explanation: The source catches up to its own wavefronts, compressing them. This produces shorter wavelengths and higher frequency ahead of the source.

questions.question: As a sound source moves forward, the wavelengths behind the source become:
questions.explanation: The source moves away from the wavefronts behind it, stretching them. This produces longer wavelengths and lower frequency behind the source.

questions.question: When an observer moves toward a stationary sound source, the observed frequency:
questions.explanation: The observer encounters more wavefronts per second, so the observed frequency increases, even though the source frequency is unchanged.

questions.question: When an observer moves away from a stationary sound source, the observed frequency:
questions.explanation: The observer encounters fewer wavefronts per second, so the observed frequency decreases.

questions.question: If both the sound source and the observer are stationary, the observed frequency is:
questions.explanation: With no relative motion, there is no Doppler shift. The observed frequency equals the source frequency.

questions.question: A train horn sounds lower in pitch as the train passes an observer and moves away because:
options: The physical amplitude of the sound waves shrinks lower over long distances.
The sound waves behind the moving train are stretched into longer wavelengths.
The train engineer reduces the physical speed of the wave after passing by.
The vibrating horn mechanism changes the actual frequency it generates.
questions.explanation: This is a classic demonstration of the Doppler effect. As the sound source moves away from the observer, each successive sound wave is emitted from a further distance. This causes the wavefronts behind the train to spread out or stretch into longer wavelengths, which results in a lower observed frequency (pitch) to the stationary listener.

questions.question: The physical speed of a sound wave traveling through the air during the Doppler effect:
options: Changes continuously depending on the absolute speed of the moving source.
Remains completely constant regardless of how fast the sound source moves.
Increases significantly as the sound source approaches a stationary observer.
Decreases gradually as the sound source passes by and begins moving away.
questions.explanation: The speed of a mechanical wave is determined entirely by the properties of the medium it travels through, such as air temperature and density. While the source's motion alters the observed wavelength and frequency (pitch), it has absolutely no effect on how fast the wave itself travels through the air.

questions.question: Police radar guns use the Doppler effect to measure a vehicle's speed by detecting:
options: The change in amplitude of reflected radio waves
The change in frequency of reflected radio waves
The change in speed of the radio waves
The temperature of the vehicle's engine
questions.explanation: Radar guns emit radio waves that reflect off the vehicle. The frequency shift between emitted and reflected waves reveals the vehicle's speed.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered the Doppler effect and its real-world applications!
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Review the difference between source motion and observer motion cases.
scoreMessages.50: Keep practicing! Focus on wavelength changes in front of and behind a moving source.
scoreMessages.0: Review the basics: approaching → higher frequency, receding → lower frequency, and why wave speed stays constant.
